Every developer remembers where they were when the orange banner of death appeared on their cloud console.
For our team, it happened on a chaotic Tuesday. We opened our browser, loaded up our cloud-based environment in Firebase Studio, and there it was — staring right back at us:
“We’re sunsetting Firebase Studio on March 22, 2027 so you can no longer create new workspaces. Move your code to Google AI Studio or Google Antigravity to continue building.”
Press enter or click to view image in full size

My team immediate reaction? Panic. Firebase Studio had been our cozy, cloud-hosted, AI-assisted safe haven. But as tech leaders, we know that platform deprecations are often blessings in disguise.
We dug deep into the official documentation and sat down with Google’s core engineering insights. What we discovered made us realize that leaving the cloud browser wasn’t a setback — it was a massive upgrade to our high-velocity engineering workflow.
Here is why we chose Google Antigravity 2.0 and exactly how we executed a flawless migration without losing our minds.
Why Antigravity? (What the Experts Won’t Tell You)
While Firebase Studio was a brilliant sandbox for rapid in-browser prototyping, it had its structural limits. According to Google’s engineering roadmap, Antigravity 2.0 is their next-generation, agent-first local desktop IDE.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Here are the three massive pillars that convinced us to make the jump:

• True Agentic Development (Multi-Agent Orchestration): Firebase Studio had a great AI chat assistant for code completion. Antigravity 2.0 introduces autonomous workflows. It doesn’t just suggest lines of code; its agents can autonomously format, run tests, manage files, and execute entire cross-functional tasks directly across your local file system.

• Local Environment Control: No more cloud container lag. Antigravity runs as a standalone desktop app. You get total authority over your local hardware, native terminals, compilers, and filesystem.

• The ‘Build with Google’ Bundle Era: Antigravity 2.0 features one-click ecosystem integrations. By enabling the Firebase Bundle, the coding agents instantly gain deep, version-aware knowledge of Firestore, Auth, and App Hosting. They can even deploy live security rules or manage users autonomously.
The Migration Guide: Step-by-Step
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The official migration path leverages Antigravity’s own agent capabilities to rewrite and adapt your project structure automatically. Before jumping in, ensure you have your local environment ready:

• Node.js (v20 or higher)

• Firebase CLI (v15.10.0 or higher)

• Google Antigravity 2.0 Desktop IDE
Step 1: Packaging and Exporting from the Cloud
First, we need to extract our source code from the cloud-based Firebase Studio instance.

• Go to your active workspace in Firebase Studio.

• Click the “Move now” button at the top header.

• If a prompt appears, click Zip and Download.
💡 Pro-Tip: If the download window fails to show up, check your browser’s address bar. Pop-up blockers are notorious for stopping this export zip. If all else fails, hit Cmd + Shift + P (or Ctrl + Shift + P on Windows/Linux) to trigger the command palette manually and run: Firebase Studio: Zip & Download.
Extract the zip file into a local folder on your machine. Ensure your folder name uses plain text without special characters or brackets, as Antigravity processes clean naming conventions much better.
Step 2: Handing the Reins to the Antigravity Agent
Now, open your freshly extracted project folder inside the Antigravity IDE and click “I trust the authors” to let the environment index your files.
Before triggering the migration, go to the right sidebar, open the model selector dropdown, and make sure you are using Gemini 3 Flash. Why? Because Flash is significantly faster for mass file conversions, allowing you to save your Pro tokens (like Gemini 3.1 Pro) for actual coding workflows later!
Once your model is selected, instruct the workspace agent to process the folder. The agent will automatically evaluate your legacy structure and prompt you with an action blueprint: Firebase Studio to Antigravity Export Plan. Click Proceed to allow the conversion.
Step 3: Resolving Non-Interactive Authentication
During the automated build, the Antigravity agent will verify your Node.js version and try to connect to your database resources. However, because the agent runs in a non-interactive runtime, it cannot manually fill in your cloud login credentials.
When the agent halts and requests manual authentication, open your terminal (as administrator) and execute:
npx firebase-tools login
• Type Y to allow CLI and Emulator suite reporting data.

• A browser window will pop up automatically. Click Allow to authenticate your Google Account.

• Once you see the "Firebase CLI Login Successful" message, close the browser window.
Go back to your Antigravity agent chat window and tell the assistant: “I have successfully authenticated my Firebase account, please proceed with the build.”
Step 4: Local Validation and Autonomous Testing
The agent will seamlessly resume, updating configuration files and restructuring dependencies in real time. Once the workspace is compiled to completion, Antigravity will automatically launch a test run inside your local browser environment.
(Note: You will notice distinct blue boundaries around your browser window — this means Antigravity is operating in autonomous agent mode, running end-to-end verifications to ensure your endpoints work beautifully natively).
Final Thoughts: The Verdict on Antigravity 2.0
Leaving Firebase Studio felt intimidating initially, but after working within a native desktop environment supercharged with autonomous multi-agent workflows, we are never looking back. Our local iterations are significantly faster, token expenditures during refactoring have plummeted, and the deep integration provided by the Firebase Bundle makes backend maintenance feel like magic.
The sunset date is March 22, 2027, but the smartest move you can make is migrating today.
Have you made the jump to Antigravity yet? Let me know your thoughts on agentic workflows in the comments below!
